result.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To solve the Polyomino Mining problem with minimal probing cost, follow this algorithmic framework: 1. Bayesian Update Mechanism: Maintain a posterior probability map P(S|Q) for each cell S being part of an oil field. When a noisy aggregate query Q (returning a count of oil cells in a region) is received, update the probabilities using Bayes' Rule: P(S|Q) proportional to P(Q|S)P(S). Since queries are noisy, model P(Q|S) as a Gaussian distribution centered on the expected number of oil cells given the current hypothesis of polyomino placements. 2. Data Structures: Bitmasks: Represent each known polyomino template as a bitmask to allow for rapid spatial shifts and logical operations. 2D Probability Grids: Maintain a grid of floating-point values representing the current belief state for every cell in the field. Template Library: A list of all possible relative offsets for each known polyomino shape. 3. Cross-Correlation Algorithm: To evaluate the likelihood of a specific polyomino placement, perform a 2D cross-correlation between the query region's bitmask and the current probability grid. The sum of products identifies areas where the template shape aligns most strongly with high-probability cells. 4. Optimal Probing Strategy: Use Information Gain (Entropy Reduction) to select drilling locations or query regions. Prioritize areas where the variance in the posterior distribution is highest. Use Drilling for high-certainty local verification and Aggregate Queries for broad spatial filtering. 5. Final Guessing: Once the probing budget is exhausted or the entropy falls below a threshold, identify all cells where the posterior probability P(S|Q) exceeds a predefined confidence threshold tau. JUSTIFICATION: This approach minimizes cost by using probabilistic inference to handle noise, bitmasks for computational efficiency, and information-theoretic selection to ensure every probe provides maximum utility toward reducing uncertainty.
